FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data used (year 2 follow-up ages 11-13, year 4 follow-up ages 13-15)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media addiction (SMA) and video game addiction (VGA) measured via adapted Bergen scales (6-item questionnaires). Applies B1: separate coefficients for VGA and SMA reported in same models.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models (LMMs) with VGA×Time and SMA×Time interaction effects; separate regression coefficients for VGA and SMA predicting neural response (beta-weights for reward/loss contrasts)

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients, 95% CI, p-values, and FDR-corrected p-values reported in Tables 3-4; example: VGA×Time interaction on loss sensitivity in left nucleus accumbens (b=0.106, 95% CI [0.040, 0.172], p=.002)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; longitudinal design with MID fMRI task data at two time points examining VGA/SMA associations with reward/loss neural processing

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Computers in Human Behavior 2025; no indication of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SM (VGA, SMA) is DEPENDENT variable in some analyses (neural responses predict later VGA/SMA), but paper also includes SM→outcome analyses (VGA/SMA→neural loss/reward sensitivity). Fails condition (a) of FT-EC7 because SM is not DEPENDENT in EVERY analysis. Additionally, covariates include non-demographic variables (neural features as predictors). Does not meet FT-EC7 binding criteria.
notes: Paper focuses on bidirectional relationships between VGA/SMA and neural reward/loss processing. VGA showed significant effects; SMA showed no significant longitudinal associations with neural sensitivity. Study design includes both directions of prediction, disqualifying from FT-EC7.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Release 5.1; data from 2-year follow-up (T2, ages 11-12) and 4-year follow-up (T4, ages 13-14)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use measured via self-report questionnaires including Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) capturing time on video games, social media, TV, phone checking, and mobile phone attachment. Problematic screen use measured via VGAQ, SMAQ, and MPIQ. Applies B1: VGAQ, SMAQ, and MPIQ reported as separate measures with own coefficients in network analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network analysis using Gaussian graphical models (Bayesian approach); cross-sectional networks derived edges represent partial correlations; cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) analyses with cross-lagged coefficients predicting psychopathology and environmental factors at T4 from screen use at T2

FT_IC4.evidence: Edge weights (partial correlations), centrality indices, p-values, and 95% CI reported in text and figures; example: 'problematic use exhibited high out-strength, which was associated with worsening psychopathologies' with specific strength values provided (e.g., T2 centrality comparisons in Fig. 2B-2E)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; cross-sectional network analysis at T2 and T4, plus cross-lagged panel network analysis examining associations between screen use and developmental outcomes (psychopathology, environmental factors)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions 2025; no indication of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes screen use as both IV and DV. Condition (a) fails: problematic screen use→outcomes (psychopathology, environmental conditions) reported, not SM-as-DV only. Additionally, cross-lagged analyses are bidirectional (B20), and network includes non-demographic predictors (behavioral tendencies, psychopathology). Does not qualify for FT-EC7.
notes: Study explicitly examines 'broader adolescent ecosystem' including family conflict, peer involvement, school participation. Problematic use showed higher centrality and stronger predictive effects than self-reported screen time alone. Cross-sectional and longitudinal network analyses both employed.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study subsample; participants wore Fitbit wearables at year 2 follow-up (ages ~12 years, mean 11.96), assessed mature screen usage at year 3 follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Mature screen usage measured via self-report: mature-rated video games and R-rated movies assessed at year 3. Applies B1: separate coefficients for mature video gaming and R-rated movie watching reported in same models (e.g., 'Shorter sleep duration...were associated with mature-rated video gaming and R-rated movie watching 1 year later')

FT_IC3.evidence: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent variables; regression coefficients for direct associations; t-tests and interaction effects examined; bidirectional associations tested via cross-lagged models. Example: 'Sensation-seeking traits were associated with R-rated movie watching 1 year later' (β=−0.07, p<.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (β), p-values, 95% CI reported in text and tables; example: 'Sensation-seeking traits were associated with R-rated movie watching 1 year later (β=−0.07, p<.001, 95% CI [−0.10, −0.03])'; sleep variables also reported with numeric values

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; prospective/longitudinal design examining associations between sleep (measured via Fitbit wearables at year 2) and sensation-seeking (year 2) with mature screen usage at year 3

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Sleep, 2025; no indication of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Mature screen use is DEPENDENT variable (predicted by sleep and sensation-seeking). However, condition (b) fails: predictors include sensation-seeking (B17 disqualifier—impulsivity/sensation-seeking trait). Condition (c) is met (longitudinal). But inclusion of sensation-seeking as predictor of SM use violates FT-EC7 criteria for demographic-only predictors. Does not qualify for FT-EC7.
notes: Study is prospective examining sleep health (objectively measured via Fitbit) and sensation-seeking as predictors of mature screen usage 1 year later. Secondary analysis examined bidirectional associations. Results show significant interaction: 'those with higher sensation-seeking scores and shorter sleep duration reported more frequent R-rated movie usage.'
